title: Building a Text Classifier: Putting It All Together
concept: An end-to-end text classifier chains everything from this unit together — raw text, cleaned by preprocessing, turned into TF-IDF vectors, fed into a Naive Bayes or Logistic Regression classifier — completing your entire 285-day classical machine learning journey.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a bigram like "not_good" capture that plain single-word counts miss?

options.text: That "not" is directly flipping the meaning of the word right next to it
The exact number of letters in the sentence
Nothing new — bigrams and unigrams always produce identical vectors

exercises.explain: Right! You now have preprocessing, N-grams, Bag of Words, and TF-IDF — every ingredient needed to build a real, working text classifier from scratch.

cards.title: One last messy folder
cards.body: Sitting in front of you: a raw folder of emails. No numbers, no clean columns — just words, exactly like the very first day of this unit. Today, you use everything from this unit, plus the classifiers you already mastered, to build a real spam filter from scratch.

cards.title: The full text classification pipeline
cards.body: An end-to-end text classifier is just this unit's ideas, chained in order: raw text → preprocessing (lowercase, strip punctuation, remove stopwords, stem/lemmatize) → vectorization (turn cleaned words, including N-grams, into TF-IDF numbers) → a classifier (Naive Bayes or Logistic Regression) → a prediction. Nothing about the classifier itself changes — only what you feed it.

cards.title: An assembly line, not one giant machine
cards.body: Think of a factory assembly line: one station cleans the raw material, the next station shapes it into a standard form, and the final station makes the decision. Raw text goes in one end of this line; a label like "spam" or "not spam" comes out the other. Each station does one simple job, and together they do something neither could do alone.

cards.title: One email, start to finish
cards.body: Raw: "WINNER!! Claim your FREE prize NOW". Preprocessed: "winner claim free prize now". TF-IDF vectorized: rare, distinctive words like "winner" and "free" get high weight; common ones get muted. That vector is fed into a trained Naive Bayes classifier — the same probability-based model from Unit 37 — which outputs "SPAM" with high confidence.

cards.title: You don't need a brand new algorithm for text
cards.body: It's tempting to think text needs some exotic, unfamiliar classifier all its own. It doesn't. Naive Bayes and Logistic Regression are the exact same algorithms from earlier in this course — the classifier never changes. Only the input representation does: once text becomes a numeric vector, it's just another set of features to learn from.

cards.title: Raw Text to Final Prediction
cards.body: The whole pipeline, start to finish:

left.text:
Raw email text
No numbers yet
Just letters and words

right.text:
Preprocessed → TF-IDF vector
→ Naive Bayes / Logistic Regression
→ Spam or Not Spam

exercises.prompt: In the full pipeline, what comes immediately before the cleaned words are handed to the classifier?

options.text: Vectorization — turning the cleaned text into numbers, such as a TF-IDF vector
Nothing — cleaned words are handed straight to the classifier as text
Deleting the classifier and picking a new one

exercises.explain: Right — vectorization is always the last stop before the classifier, since Naive Bayes and Logistic Regression can only compute with numbers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To classify text, Naive Bayes has to become a completely different algorithm than the one used on numeric data earlier in the course.
exercises.explain: False — it's the exact same Naive Bayes. Only the input changes: once text is vectorized into numbers, the classifier works exactly as it always has.

exercises.sentence: The correct pipeline order is: raw text → preprocessing → ___ → classifier → prediction.
exercises.explain: Vectorization is the step that finally turns cleaned words into the numeric features a classifier needs.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes a working end-to-end text classifier?

options.text: Raw text is cleaned, turned into a TF-IDF vector, then fed into a trained Naive Bayes or Logistic Regression model to get a prediction
Raw text is fed directly into Logistic Regression with no cleaning or vectorization at all
A brand new, never-before-seen algorithm is required just for text, unrelated to anything learned earlier

exercises.explain: Exactly — every piece of this unit exists to feed the classifiers you already know a numeric representation they can actually learn from.

exercises.prompt: You've now taken raw, messy email text all the way through preprocessing and TF-IDF vectorization, and into a trained classifier to build a real spam filter — using the exact same Naive Bayes and Logistic Regression algorithms you mastered earlier in the course. This is Day 285: the final lesson of the entire classical machine learning journey. What single idea connects everything you've learned, from Day 1 all the way to today?

options.text: Almost any real-world problem — numbers, categories, or even raw text — can be turned into a clean numeric representation that a classical machine learning model can learn from and make a real decision with
Every problem needs its own brand new algorithm invented from scratch
Text data is fundamentally impossible for classical machine learning to ever handle
None of the earlier units actually connect to this one

exercises.explain: 🎉 That's it. That is the whole course, in one idea. From Day 1's "What is AI?" through regression, classification, clustering, ensembles, your churn predictor capstone, and now — finally — text: 56 units, 285 lessons, every single one teaching you a new way to turn a messy real-world problem into numbers a model can learn from. You can now take data in practically any form it arrives in and carry it all the way to a working prediction. Congratulations — you've completed the entire classical machine learning curriculum. 🎓
